content: Persian Empire under Cyrus the Great conquered the Greek cities of Ionia in western Anatolia
context: The Ionian Greeks lost their independence to Persia around 547 BC. They chafed under Persian-appointed tyrants, setting the stage for rebellion.

content: Ionian Revolt — Greek cities in Anatolia rebelled against Persian rule with Athenian support
context: Athens and Eretria sent ships to support the revolt. The rebels burned Sardis, the Persian regional capital. Darius I vowed revenge against Athens specifically.

content: Darius I decided to punish Athens for supporting the Ionian Revolt, launching the first Persian invasion of Greece
context: Darius reportedly had a servant tell him at every dinner: 'Master, remember the Athenians.' The expedition was a punitive campaign targeting Athens and Eretria specifically.

content: Greek hoplite phalanx — heavy infantry formation using overlapping shields and long spears in tight ranks
context: The phalanx depended on cohesion and discipline. Hoplites carried a large round shield (aspis), spear, and short sword. The formation was nearly impenetrable from the front but vulnerable on flanks and rear.

content: Battle of Marathon — 10,000 Athenians defeated 25,000 Persians through superior tactics and terrain use
context: Miltiades convinced the generals to attack. The Athenians weakened their center and strengthened the wings, executing a double envelopment. 6,400 Persians died vs 192 Athenians. Pheidippides ran 26 miles to announce the victory.

content: Themistocles persuaded Athens to build a 200-ship navy using silver mine revenues, transforming Athens into a naval power
context: A massive silver strike at Laurium provided the funds. Themistocles argued the fleet was needed against Aegina, but his true vision was defense against Persia. This decision saved Greece.

content: Xerxes I assembled the largest army the ancient world had ever seen to conquer Greece — estimated 100,000-300,000 troops
context: Herodotus claimed 1.7 million, but modern estimates are 100,000-300,000. Xerxes built a pontoon bridge across the Hellespont and a canal through Mount Athos. The scale of logistics was unprecedented.

content: Battle of Thermopylae — King Leonidas and 300 Spartans held the narrow pass for three days against the Persian army
context: 7,000 Greeks (not just 300) initially defended the pass. Leonidas dismissed most allies when outflanked via a mountain path betrayed by Ephialtes. The 300 Spartans, 700 Thespians, and 400 Thebans fought to the death.

content: Leonidas chose to stay and die at Thermopylae rather than retreat, buying time for Greek mobilization
context: The sacrifice at Thermopylae became the defining moment of Greek resistance. It delayed the Persian advance and inspired all of Greece to unite against the invader. 'Go tell the Spartans...'

content: Xerxes sacked and burned Athens after the city was evacuated on Themistocles' order
context: Themistocles interpreted the Delphic Oracle's 'wooden walls' prophecy as referring to ships, not the Acropolis walls. He convinced Athenians to evacuate to Salamis. The Persians destroyed the empty city.

content: Battle of Salamis — Themistocles lured the Persian fleet into the narrow straits where Greek triremes had the advantage
context: Themistocles sent a false message to Xerxes claiming Greeks would flee, inducing the Persians to attack in the narrow strait. The larger Persian ships could not maneuver. 300 Persian ships sunk vs 40 Greek.

content: Xerxes retreated to Asia after Salamis, leaving general Mardonius with a land army in Greece
context: The destruction of the fleet meant Xerxes could not supply his massive army by sea. He returned to Persia with most of his forces, leaving 300,000 troops (likely fewer) under Mardonius.

content: Battle of Plataea — combined Greek army of 80,000 destroyed the remaining Persian land forces
context: Spartan-led coalition defeated Mardonius, who was killed in battle. The victory ended the Persian invasion of mainland Greece permanently. Greek hoplites proved superior in close combat.

content: Battle of Mycale — Greek fleet destroyed the Persian fleet on the same day as Plataea, liberating Ionia
context: The coordinated victories on land and sea ended Persian control of the Aegean. Ionian Greek cities were liberated, completing the cycle that began with the Ionian Revolt.

content: Delian League formed under Athenian leadership to continue the war against Persia and prevent future invasions
context: Greek city-states contributed ships or money to a common treasury at Delos. Athens gradually transformed the league into an empire, using its navy to enforce tribute collection.

content: Greek trireme naval warfare relied on ramming tactics and superior seamanship in narrow waters
context: The trireme was powered by 170 oarsmen in three tiers. Greek tactics favored the diekplous (sailing through gaps to ram) and periplous (outflanking). Speed and maneuverability trumped numbers.
